title: Carbon Capture Utilisation and Storage - Energy System - IEA
gist: IEA's status review of global CCUS as of April 2024: ~45 commercial facilities capture >50 Mt CO2/year with billions in US, EU, Dutch, Danish, German and Japanese funding flowing, but only ~20% of announced 2030 capacity has reached FID and the project pipeline sits at just ~40% of the Net Zero Scenario need — a sector with strong policy momentum but a large execution gap.

insights.claim: US and European subsidy programmes made over USD 20 billion available to CCUS projects in 2023, including USD 1.7 billion for capture demonstrations and USD 1.2 billion for direct air capture hubs under the 2021 US Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A >$20bn public funding floor de-risks early CCUS capex and underpins the project pipeline for equipment and engineering vendors.
insights.quote: made over USD 20 billion available to CCUS projects
insights.locator: Public funding: Government funding for CCUS continued in 2023

insights.claim: The Netherlands committed over USD 7.3 billion to CCS via its SDE++ scheme (feeding the Aramis network) and Denmark's Ørsted received USD 1.2 billion from the CCUS Fund for its Asnæs power-station capture retrofit.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Northern Europe is emerging as the densest CCS funding cluster — names like Ørsted and the Aramis/Porthos networks are the concrete vehicles.
insights.quote: the SDE++ scheme allocated over USD 7.3 billion to CCS projects
insights.locator: In the Netherlands, the SDE++ scheme

insights.claim: Germany re-entered CCUS via a carbon management strategy naming it crucial to 2045 carbon neutrality, while Japan selected seven large-scale projects to capture ~13 Mt CO2/year by 2030 and is advancing a CCS Business Act.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Germany and Japan reversing/accelerating policy stances widens the addressable CCUS market into two major industrial economies.
insights.quote: Germany made its re-entry into CCUS
insights.locator: Germany made its re-entry into CCUS

insights.claim: Around 45 commercial capture facilities operate globally with total annual capacity over 50 Mt CO2, yet the current project pipeline is only around 40% of the Net Zero Scenario requirement for 2030.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: The 40%-of-NZE pipeline quantifies the build-out shortfall — a structural under-delivery risk for climate-aligned CCUS scenarios.
insights.quote: the pipeline of current projects is only just around 40% of the Net Zero Scenario requirement in 2030
insights.locator: There are now around 45 commercial capture facilities

insights.claim: As of February 2024 only ~20% of announced 2030 capture capacity was in operation or had reached final investment decision, exposing a wide gap between announcements and committed projects.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: The 80% of announced capacity lacking FID is the credibility discount to apply to CCUS pipeline headlines.
insights.quote: still accounts for just 20% of announced capture capacity for 2030
insights.locator: Announcements are however just the first step

insights.claim: Industry (cement, steel) makes up less than 10% of announced CCUS capacity but would need to reach a quarter of all CO2 captured by 2030 in the Net Zero Scenario — the most under-represented hard-to-abate segment.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Industrial CCUS is the biggest under-built gap relative to need — a policy/technology white space for cement and steel decarbonization plays.
insights.quote: particularly industry, which currently makes up less than 10% of announced capacity
insights.locator: But greater ambition is needed in some sectors

themes.theme: Application mix shifting from gas processing

insights.claim: Around 65% of operating CO2 capture capacity today is at natural-gas processing plants (one of the lowest-cost applications), but economy-wide 2050 decarbonization targets are diversifying capture toward hydrogen, power, industry and direct air capture.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: The shift from cheap gas-processing capture to higher-cost applications raises average project cost and dependence on subsidy/carbon pricing.
insights.quote: around 65% of operating CO2 capture capacity is at natural gas processing plants
insights.locator: Currently, around 65% of operating CO2 capture capacity

insights.claim: Based on the current pipeline, by 2030 annual capture could reach ~95 Mt CO2 from hydrogen production, ~90 Mt from power generation, ~50 Mt from industry and ~65 Mt from DAC plants.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Hydrogen and power lead the projected 2030 capture mix — aligns CCUS demand with the low-carbon hydrogen and gas-power build-out.
insights.locator: Based on the current project pipeline, by 2030

insights.claim: CCUS projects are now in development across more than 50 countries, with China adding four capture facilities in 2023 and the Middle East running ~15 projects in development plus ADNOC's FID on a 1.5 Mt CO2/year capture facility at Habshan-Bab.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: National oil companies (ADNOC) and China entering CCUS broadens the contractor and storage-asset opportunity beyond the West.
insights.quote: projects now being developed in more than 50 countries
insights.locator: The geographic distribution of CO2 capture projects

insights.claim: Novel capture approaches are being demonstrated, including NET Power's Allam-cycle gas plant using CO2 as a working fluid and the UK's Net Zero Teesside Power (online expected 2027) as a potential first commercial-scale gas plant with CCUS.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Allam-cycle and first-of-kind commercial gas+CCUS plants are the technology bets to watch for capture-cost step-changes.
insights.quote: a first-of-its-kind natural gas-fired power plant employing Allam cycle technology
insights.locator: NET Power's 50 MW clean energy plant

insights.claim: Chemical-absorption results show CO2 capture rates as high as 99% are achievable at comparably low additional marginal cost relative to standard 90% capture, countering assumptions that very high capture is prohibitively expensive.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Near-total capture at low incremental cost weakens the 'residual emissions' critique of CCUS and supports its net-zero role.
insights.quote: CO2 capture rates as high as 99% can be achieved at comparably low additional marginal cost
insights.locator: However, initial results based on chemical absorption

themes.theme: Hubs, lead times and business models

insights.claim: CCUS infrastructure projects can take up to ten years to develop across multiple stakeholders and regulatory regimes, and the IEA warns long lead times put short-term climate targets at risk unless permitting is streamlined.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Permitting and lead-time bottlenecks are the practical brake on CCUS scale-up — one-stop-shop regulation is the catalyst to watch.
insights.quote: CCUS projects ... can take up to ten years to be developed
insights.locator: CCUS projects are large infrastructure endeavours

insights.claim: New 'as-a-service' and part-chain CCUS business models — where capture, transport and storage are offered as third-party services and hubs share infrastructure costs across emitters — are emerging to accelerate scale-up.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Unbundled CO2-management-as-a-service mirrors infrastructure utility models — a structural shift in how CCUS value chains get financed.
insights.quote: developing "as-a-service" business models for CO2 capture, transport and storage
insights.locator: New business models and deployment approaches

insights.claim: Multiple North Sea CO2 cross-border transport arrangements were signed in 2024 (Denmark, Belgium, Netherlands, Sweden each with Norway; Denmark-France) under the London Protocol, while Japan signed a transboundary CO2 MOU with Malaysia's PETRONAS.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: An emerging legal framework for cross-border CO2 shipping creates a new international transport-and-storage market, with Norway and PETRONAS as storage hosts.
insights.quote: each established an arrangement on cross-border transport of CO2 with Norway in April 2024
